hypothesis.assumption: Let (a_n) be a real sequence and let L \in \mathbb{R} satisfy the following property: for every \varepsilon > 0 there exists N \in \mathbb{N} such that for every n \ge N there exists m \ge n with |a_m - L| < \varepsilon.

proof.text: We prove the two desired inequalities separately, using the standard definitions of limsup and liminf in terms of suprema and infima of tails of the sequence.

proof.statement: For each n \in \mathbb{N} define s_n := \sup\{ a_k : k \ge n \}.
For each n \in \mathbb{N} define i_n := \inf\{ a_k : k \ge n \}.

proof.claim: The sequence (s_n) is decreasing and the sequence (i_n) is increasing.
proof.proof_method: This follows from the fact that as n increases, the tail sets {a_k : k \ge n} become smaller, so their suprema form a decreasing sequence and their infima form an increasing sequence.

proof.label: eq:limsup-liminf-def
proof.proof_method: This is the standard definition of limsup and liminf via suprema and infima of the tails of the sequence.

proof.text: We first prove the inequality L \le \limsup_{n \to \infty} a_n.

proof.label: goal:L-le-s_n
proof.claim: For every n \in \mathbb{N}, one has L \le s_n.
proof.proof_method: We prove this by contradiction using the hypothesis on (a_n) and L.

proof.label: contra:L-gt-s_n
proof.assumption: There exists n_0 \in \mathbb{N} such that L > s_{n_0}.

proof.statement: Fix such an index and denote it by n = n_0.
Let E_n := \{ a_k : k \ge n \}, so that s_n = \sup E_n.

proof.proof_method: This follows directly from the assumption L > s_n.

proof.claim: There exists N \in \mathbb{N} such that for every p \ge N there exists m \ge p with |a_m - L| < \delta/2.
proof.proof_method: Apply the hypothesis on (a_n) and L to the chosen \varepsilon = \delta/2.

proof.statement: Set p := \max\{N, n\}, so that p \ge N and p \ge n.
By the choice of N, there exists m \ge p such that |a_m - L| < \delta/2.

proof.claim: a_m \in E_n and hence a_m \le s_n.
proof.proof_method: Since m \ge p \ge n, we have m \ge n, so a_m is in E_n; by definition of s_n as sup E_n, a_m \le s_n.

proof.label: ineq:am-between
proof.proof_method: This is equivalent to the inequality |a_m - L| < \delta/2.

proof.label: ineq:am-lower
proof.proof_method: Substitute \delta = L - s_n into L - \delta/2 < a_m to obtain L - (L - s_n)/2 < a_m, which simplifies to (L + s_n)/2 < a_m.
Combine (L + s_n)/2 < a_m with a_m \le s_n.
Multiply (L + s_n)/2 < s_n by 2 to obtain L + s_n < 2 s_n, hence L < s_n.

proof.claim: L > s_n and L < s_n is a contradiction.
proof.proof_method: The assumption gave L > s_n, while the previous step yielded L < s_n.

proof.claim: The assumption that there exists n with L > s_n is false, so for every n \in \mathbb{N} we have L \le s_n.
proof.proof_method: Proof by contradiction: since assuming L > s_n for some n leads to a contradiction, we conclude L \le s_n for all n.

results_used.statement: For every n, L \le s_n, so L is a lower bound of the set {s_n : n \in \mathbb{N}}; therefore L \le \inf_{n \in \mathbb{N}} s_n = \limsup_{n \to \infty} a_n.

proof.text: We now prove the inequality \liminf_{n \to \infty} a_n \le L.

proof.label: goal:i_n-le-L
proof.claim: For every n \in \mathbb{N}, one has i_n \le L.
proof.proof_method: Again we argue by contradiction using the hypothesis on (a_n) and L.

proof.label: contra:i_n-gt-L
proof.assumption: There exists n_1 \in \mathbb{N} such that i_{n_1} > L.

proof.statement: Fix such an index and denote it by n = n_1.
Let E_n := \{ a_k : k \ge n \}, so that i_n = \inf E_n.

proof.proof_method: This follows directly from the assumption i_n > L.

proof.claim: There exists N \in \mathbb{N} such that for every p \ge N there exists m \ge p with |a_m - L| < \eta/2.
proof.proof_method: Apply the hypothesis on (a_n) and L to the chosen \varepsilon = \eta/2.

proof.statement: Set p := \max\{N, n\}, so that p \ge N and p \ge n.
By the choice of N, there exists m \ge p such that |a_m - L| < \eta/2.

proof.claim: a_m \in E_n and hence i_n \le a_m.
proof.proof_method: Since m \ge p \ge n, we have m \ge n, so a_m is in E_n; by definition of i_n as inf E_n, i_n \le a_m.

proof.label: ineq:am-between-eta
proof.proof_method: This is equivalent to the inequality |a_m - L| < \eta/2.

proof.label: ineq:am-upper
proof.proof_method: Substitute \eta = i_n - L into a_m < L + \eta/2 to obtain a_m < L + (i_n - L)/2 = (L + i_n)/2.
Combine i_n \le a_m with a_m < (L + i_n)/2.
From i_n \le a_m < (L + i_n)/2, deduce i_n < (L + i_n)/2.
Multiply i_n < (L + i_n)/2 by 2 to get 2 i_n < L + i_n, hence i_n < L.

proof.claim: i_n > L and i_n < L is a contradiction.
proof.proof_method: The assumption gave i_n > L, while the previous step yielded i_n < L.

proof.claim: The assumption that there exists n with i_n > L is false, so for every n \in \mathbb{N} we have i_n \le L.
proof.proof_method: Proof by contradiction: since assuming i_n > L for some n leads to a contradiction, we conclude i_n \le L for all n.

results_used.statement: For every n, i_n \le L, so L is an upper bound of the set {i_n : n \in \mathbb{N}}; therefore \sup_{n \in \mathbb{N}} i_n \le L, i.e., \liminf_{n \to \infty} a_n = \sup_{n \in \mathbb{N}} i_n \le L.
Combine the inequalities L \le \limsup_{n \to \infty} a_n and \liminf_{n \to \infty} a_n \le L.
